coredata.dc:title: Economical, green and rapid synthesis of CDs-Cu2O/CuO nanotube from the biomass waste reed as sensitive sensing platform for the electrochemical detection of hydrazine
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Guili
Wang, Lei
Huo, Li
Zhang, Yufan
coredata.dc:description: Reeds live widely in swamp and wetland and have an important ecological balance functions in environmental protection. However, the use of reeds is not effectively. The carbon dots (CDs) have been developed as the family of 0D nanocarbon materials and have all the advantages of the carbon family. In this work, we prepared CDs via hydrothermal method using reed. It is surprising that the reed-derived CDs are an effective reducing agent. A highly catalytically active composite material CDs-Cu2O/CuO was synthesized using economical, green, one-step ultrasonic method and used for the detection of hydrazine. The electrochemical detection of hydrazine was investigated by cyclic voltammetry. The result shows that the CDs-Cu2O/CuO exhibited good electrocatalytic activity for the oxidation of hydrazine. The fabricated sensor presented a wide linear range from 0.99 μM to 5903 μM and a detection limit of 0.024 μM. In addition, the CDs-Cu2O/CuO exhibited good sensitivity, stability and repeatability. Therefore, the CDs-Cu2O/CuO provides a new idea for the utilization of reed and low-cost electrocatalytic nanocomposite.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Reed
CDs-Cu2O/CuO composite
Electrochemical detection
Hydrazine

Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FT-IR spectra of CDs and CDs-Cu2O/CuO; (B) XRD spectra of CDs and CDs-Cu2O/CuO.
(A) XPS full spectra; (B) C1s spectra; (C) O1s spectra; (D) Cu2p spectra of CDs-Cu2O/CuO.
(A) TEM and HRTEM image of CDs; (B)TEM and HRTEM image of CDs-Cu2O/CuO; (C) SEM image of CDs-Cu2O/CuO; (D) STEM image of CDs-Cu2O/CuO with elemental mapping of C, O, Cu.

(A) CV of Bare-GCE; (B) CDs-GCE; (C) CDs-Cu2O-GCE; (D) CDs-Cu2O/CuO-GCE (in the blank (black line) and presence (red line) of hydrazine) (0.2 mM) in NaOH solutions (0.1 M). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
CDs-Cu2O/CuO-GCE with different scan rate (50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 100, 150, 200 mV s−1) of hydrazine (0.2 mM) in NaOH solution (0.1 M).

Chronoamperometric of CDs-Cu2O/CuO-GCE in different concentration of hydrazine (0 μM, 5 μM, 10 μM, 15 μM, 20 μM) in NaOH solution (0.1 M) (the inset of a: the linear relationship between I cat/I L and t1/2; inset b: the linear relationship between I and t−1/2).

(A) Current-time curve of CDs-Cu2O/CuO-GCE with additions of hydrazine (the inset of a: current-time curve of CDs-Cu2O/CuO-GCE with lower concentration of hydrazine); (B) The linear relationship between the different concentration of hydrazine and the current response for CDs-Cu2O/CuO-GCE (the inset of b: a linear relationship between the lower concentration of hydrazine and the current response for CDs-Cu2O/CuO-GCE).
Current-time curve of CDs-Cu2O/CuO-GCE with addition of several interfering substance or hydrazine.
Reduction mechanism for the preparation of CDs-Cu2O/CuO composite using CDs as reducing agent.
Comparison of the linear range (μM), sensitivity (μA mM−1) and LOD (μM) of CDs-Cu2O/CuO with other modified electrodes.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Ag/Zn organic frameworks nanocomposites modified GCE.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: CuOdecorated on the ordered mesoporous carbon modified GCE.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Co3O4 nanoparticles modified on the multi-walled carbon nanotubes modified GCE.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Nickel ferrite decorated on multi-walled carbon nanotubes modified GCE.

head.ce:title: Economical, green and rapid synthesis of CDs-Cu2O/CuO nanotube from the biomass waste reed as sensitive sensing platform for the electrochemical detection of hydrazine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemistry and Molecular Diagnosis of Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Environmental Science, Hebei University, Baoding, 071002, Hebei, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemistry and Molecular Diagnosis of Ministry of Education
College of Chemistry and Environmental Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemistry and Molecular Diagnosis of Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Environmental Science, Hebei University, Baoding, 071002, Hebei, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reeds live widely in swamp and wetland and have an important ecological balance functions in environmental protection. However, the use of reeds is not effectively. The carbon dots (CDs) have been developed as the family of 0D nanocarbon materials and have all the advantages of the carbon family. In this work, we prepared CDs via hydrothermal method using reed. It is surprising that the reed-derived CDs are an effective reducing agent. A highly catalytically active composite material CDs-Cu2O/CuO was synthesized using economical, green, one-step ultrasonic method and used for the detection of hydrazine. The electrochemical detection of hydrazine was investigated by cyclic voltammetry. The result shows that the CDs-Cu2O/CuO exhibited good electrocatalytic activity for the oxidation of hydrazine. The fabricated sensor presented a wide linear range from 0.99 μM to 5903 μM and a detection limit of 0.024 μM. In addition, the CDs-Cu2O/CuO exhibited good sensitivity, stability and repeatability. Therefore, the CDs-Cu2O/CuO provides a new idea for the utilization of reed and low-cost electrocatalytic nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:para: With the shortage of energy resources, the use of some renewable resources has caused widespread attention. Reed is called as “second forest” which has many ecological functions such as regulating climate, purifying sewage and maintaining biodiversity. Reed is also one of the main biomass waste growing in wetland and swamp. However, the utilization of high value-added reed is still rare. At present, Jiang et al. [1] used a natural reed film as a precursor to prepare a unique porous layer of activated carbon (RHC), and a graphene-like RHC was prepared by hydrothermal treatment and carbonization.

Metal oxides such as Cu2O and CuO exhibiting catalytic ability have recently received much attention [2–6]. Cu2O and CuO are p-type semiconductor materials, which have the advantages of low cost, excellent electrical conductivity and high catalytic efficiency. Cu2O and CuO are promising materials in solar conversion, sensing, photocatalytic degradation, electrocatalytic degradation and in lithiumion batteries, etc [7–12]. At present, many researchers pay attention to the researches of copper oxide. Gu et al. [11] reported that the Cu2O had excellent electro catalytic reduction of p-nitrophenol. Wang et al. [12] prepared the CuO/GCE for detection of hydrazine, the layered copper oxide had an expanded structure which can improve the ability of the transfer of electrons [13]. Hu et al. [4] reported that a novel CuO/Cu2O hollow polyhedron with reversible lithium storage capacity was prepared by thermal decomposition. Yu et al. [14] synthetized the CuO/Cu2O hollow microspheres by the hydrothermal method, which exhibited the excellent photocatalytic activity. CuO/Cu2O bi-component metal oxides can exhibit a strong synergistic effect and enhance the unique properties of each component [2]. Copper oxide is usually synthesized with hydrazine, NaBH4 and dimethylhydrazine as reducing agents [15,16]. Among reducing agents, a trace amount of hydrazine is harmful to lungs and kidneys and causes carcinogenic [15]. So, the establishment of a rapid and accurate detection of hydrazine and the synthesis of green reducing agents have attracted extensive attention.

CDs have recently become a new family of 0D nanocarbon [17,18]. CDs can be widely derived from nature biomass, such as rose flower, bamboo leaf cellulose, carnation, orange juice, grass, soy, zucchini, porphyry, ramie, etc [17–23]. Many sustainable carbon-based materials are used for the synthesis of CDs because these materials are environmentally friendly and economically viable materials. Most of CDs are used in the field of fluorescent ion detection, cell imaging, etc. [24–26], and have also been used for the synthesis of new materials as the reducing agent. Konwar et al. [27] prepared CDs from tea to reduce graphene oxide, and the composite material was applied to highly flexible cotton-based conductive fabrics. Shen et al. [28] used CDs as a reducing agent to directly reduce Ag+ to Ag in a water bath. So, the bio-based CDs have the highest possibility to become the suitable candidate for other reducing agents due to low cost, non-toxic.

In this work, the one-step simply synthesize of reed based CDs via hydrothermal method, which makes full use of agricultural waste and gives a new research idea for reed. At the same time, we propose the synthesis of the CDs-Cu2O/CuO composite via an ecofriendly and cost-effective ultrasonic method using CDs as a reducing agent. The fabricated sensor was applied in the field of electrochemical detection of hydrazine. The electrocatalytic result showed that the CDs-Cu2O/CuO composite exhibited excellent electrocatalytic ability with low limit of detection, high sensitivity and wide linear range.

Reed was picked in Baiyangdian (China). Cupric sulfate (CuSO4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Tianjin Commio Chemical Testing Co., Ltd. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, average MW 45000) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochenical Co., Ltd. The 0.1 M NaOH (pH = 13.0) as the supporting electrolyte.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) of samples were recorded on a Bruker TENSOR-27 spectrometer in the range from 400 to 4000 cm−1 using KBr pellets at 25 °C. X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD) of samples were obtained using Bruker D8-ADVANCE in the range of 10–80°. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electronmicroscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained using FEI Tecnai G2F20 microscope (FEI, US) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Cold field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained using JSM-7500 at a magnification of 10000 times. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were performed using a monochromatic Al-Kα X-ray source (hʋ = 1486.6 eV). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of samples were obtained using PerkinElmer Diamond TG analyzer. The electrochemical experiments were obtained using CHI760E electrochemical Analyzer and AUTOLAB electrochemical workstation. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) images were obtained using the Autolab electrochemical analyzer.

The reed powder (10 g) and deionized water (120 mL) were added into a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave (250 mL), and then heated to 180 °C for 3 h. The mixture was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min. The CDs powder was obtained by dried under vacuum for 48 h at 50 °C and was formulated into a 2 mg mL−1 solution for later use.

NaOH (30 mL/1 M) was slowly dripped into CuSO4 (30 mL/0.1 M) with magnetic stirring and then sonicated (150 W, 40 KHz) for 20 min. Then, CDs (40 mL/2 mg mL−1) were slowly dripped into the mixture solution with magnetic stirring and ultrasonic treatment for 60 min, and the mixed solution was aged for 24 h. The mixture was washed with deionized water and absolute ethyl alcohol for several times and was dried in vacuum at 60 °C for 3 h. Finally, the CDs-Cu2O composite material was obtained.

NaOH (30 mL/1 M) was slowly dripped into CuSO4 (30 mL/0.1 M) with magnetic stirring and then sonicated (150 W, 40 KHz) for 20 min, and followed by adding 3 mL PVP (50 g L−1) solution to it. Then, CDs (40 mL/2 mg mL−1) were slowly dripped into the mixture solution with magnetic stirring and ultrasonic treatment for 60 min, and the mixed solution was aged for 24 h. The mixture was washed with deionized water and absolute ethyl alcohol for several times and was dried in vacuum at 60 °C for 3 h. Finally, the CDs-Cu2O/CuO composite material was obtained.

Before modifying the electrode, the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was polished with alumina powder, and then ultrasonically irradiated with 1:1 nitric acid and H2O, absolute ethanol and H2O for 3 min. The electrode surface was cleaned by deionized water for use. The catalyst sample was dispersed into a 2 mg mL−1 solution, and 5 μL of the suspension was applied to the surface of the GCE, followed by drying for use.

The CDs-Cu2O/CuO composite was prepared by the reduction of Cu2+ using CDs. In this reduction process, CDs as the reducing agent can reduce Cu2+ due to many hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface of CDs. Among them, the hydroxyl of CDs can reduce Cu2+ by a polyphenolic mechanism, and the carbonyl groups reduce Cu2+ by Benedict reaction [26]. The reduction mechanism is shown in Scheme 1 . The FT-IR spectra (in Fig. 1 A) of the CDs-Cu2O/CuO composite exhibit the peak at 480 cm−1 ascribed to the Cu (I, II)–O stretching vibration. The peak at 1620 cm−1 corresponding to the CC bending vibration which confirms that CDs contain C [29]. The typical hydroxyl peak of the CDs-Cu2O/CuO at 3430 cm−1 is significantly lower than that of CDs, indicating that –OH and Cu2+ react on the surface of CDs. Simultaneously, the typical peak of CO at 1645 cm−1 is obviously higher than CDs because CO and Cu2+ interact on the surface of CDs. The above peaks confirm that the obtained composite has C and Cu oxide. The powder XRD spectra of the composite shows that all the peaks can be assigned to Cu2O (JCPDS card: 78–2076) and CuO (JCPDS card: 80–1917), which corresponds to the FT-IR spectra that Cu–O in the composite is Cu2O and CuO in Fig. 1B [4]. The TG analysis of the CDs and the CDs-Cu2O/CuO in air are shown in Fig. S1. It can be clearly seen that CDs have significant weight loss relative to the CDs-Cu2O/CuO, which confirms that the composite have some substances that are not easily decomposed. The obtained composite weight decrease slowly between 35 and 200 °C, which may be due to the removal of chemisorbed H2O. The composite displays a sharp weight loss between 200 and 450 °C, which may be due to the removal of residual PVP in the samples [4]. The weight of the composite increased after 450 °C, which confirms the conversion of Cu2O to CuO.

The different functional groups of the CDs-Cu2O/CuO composite was investigated by the XPS analysis (Fig. 2 A–D). The XPS full spectra is shown that the composite contain C element, which can indicate the existence of CDs in the composite (Fig. 2A). The peaks at 284.57, 285.6, 286.4, 287.3, 288.26 eV in the C1s spectra (Fig. 2B) of CDs demonstrate the presence of CC, C–O, C–O–C, CO and O–CO, respectively [30]. Comparing the C1s of the CDs-Cu2O/CuO to CDs, it is shown that the C1s spectra has not change significantly. The peak at 529.6 eV in the O1s spectra of the CDs-Cu2O/CuO (Fig. 2C) indicates the existence of different metal-oxygen in the composite [4]. There have two shake-up peaks at 962.44 eV and 942.38 eV, which demonstrate the existence of Cu2+ (Fig. 2D) [31]. Simultaneously, there have two peaks at 934.15 eV and 954.51 eV, which correspond to the Cu2p1/2 and Cu2p3/2 of Cu2+, respectively. Moreover, the peak at 935.51 eV indicates the existence of Cu+, which indicates the existence of Cu2O in the composite [32]. Hence, the FT-IR, XRD, TG and XPS analysis can prove the existence of CuO and Cu2O in the CDs-Cu2O/CuO. The XPS of other elements in CDs have been supplemented in Fig. S2. The contents of N and P elements are extremely low, which have the extremely weak electrocatalysis. Simultaneously, the elemental contents of CDs and CDs-Cu2O/CuO obtained from XPS analysis are shown in Table S1.

Fig. 3 A shows the TEM images of CDs. Fig. S3 shows the different magnifications of CDs. The interlayer distance is 0.20 nm (Fig. 3A) and the average diameter of CDs is 2.7 nm (Fig. S4). TEM image of the composite is obtained with a randomly selected particle (Fig. 3B). The interlayer distances of 0.25 nm and 0.27 nm agree well with the (111) and (110) crystal planes of Cu2O and CuO, respectively (Fig. 3B) [4]. Fig. 3C shows the SEM image of the CDs-Cu2O/CuO composite at a low magnification, which displays nanotube structure. Fig. S5 shows the different magnifications of the CDs-Cu2O/CuO composite. Mapping analysis demonstrates that the composites are distribution of C, O and Cu elements in Fig. 3D. It also can be concluded from the analysis of EDS that the main elements of the CDs-Cu2O/CuO composite are C, O and Cu (Fig. S6).

Fig. 4 shows the CV curves of hydrazine on bare GCE, CDs-GCE, CDs-Cu2O-GCE, and CDs-Cu2O/CuO-GCE, respectively. Fig. 4A and B show that CDs-GCE has a higher electrochemical response for hydrazine than bare GCE, which shows that CDs can improve the electrochemical response of hydrazine oxidation. However, compared CDs-Cu2O-GCE to CDs-Cu2O/CuO-GCE, CDs-Cu2O/CuO-GCE has a higher electrochemical response current for hydrazine in Fig. 4C and D. Therefore, CDs-Cu2O/CuO-GCE is selected as an excellent sensor for electrochemical detection of hydrazine. The electrical conductivity of different electrodes was investigated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) experiments. Fig. S7 illustrates the EIS results for bare GCE, CDs-GCE, CDs-Cu2O-GCE and CDs-Cu2O/CuO-GCE, respectively. The values of the electron transfer resistance (R ct) of different electrode materials were obtained by fitting the data in Table S2.

Fig. 5 shows that the current response increases with the scanning rate increases. The result shows that CDs-Cu2O/CuO-GCE depends on the scanning rate. A good linear relationship between the current intensity and the square root of scan rate (v 1/2) with correlation coefficient (R2) 0.99386. And the linear relationship illustrates the catalytic oxidation of hydrazine into a diffusion control process. At the same time, with the scanning rate increases, the oxidation peak potential shift is small, which indicates that the electron transfer kinetics of the catalyst surface is very fast. Therefore, the electrochemical oxidation of hydrazine by chronoamperometry was further studied. Fig. 6 shows the current response of CDs-Cu2O/CuO with the concentration of hydrazine increases. The current response of CDs and CDs-Cu2O are shown in Fig. S8. According to the formula calculation can be drawn the catalytic rate constant (k cat) and the diffusion coefficient (D). The k cat can be received according to the equation:
I
cat
/
I
L
=
(
π
k
cat
ct
)
1 / 2
where the I cat is the catalytic current at a certain hydrazine concentration; the I L is the background current corresponding to a certain concentration of hydrazine; the c is the concentration of hydrazine at a certain moment. The catalytic rate constant (k cat) of CDs, CDs-Cu2O and CDs-Cu2O/CuO were investigated by chronoamperometry experiments. The result shows that the k cat of CDs-Cu2O/CuO, CDs and CDs-Cu2O are 4.90 × 104 M−1 s−1, 3.98 × 104 M−1 s−1 and 9.21 × 102 M−1 s−1, respectively. As can be seen, CDs-Cu2O/CuO has a higher electrocatalytic rate constant and a higher electrocatalytic activity. The k cat of CDs-Cu2O/CuO is higher than that of the previous report [33–35]. So CDs-Cu2O/CuO is more preferred for electrocatalytic activity toward the oxidation of hydrazine.

Fig. 7 shows that the current-time curve was obtained by continuously increasing the concentration of hydrazine over time, and the current-time curve of hydrazine. According to the change of the current response of CDs-Cu2O/CuO-GCE to hydrazine at low concentration (0.99 μM), it proves that CDs-Cu2O/CuO-GCE can be as an excellent detection sensor for hydrazine. As can be seen from the Fig. 7A, the current response of hydrazine is immediately changing with the different concentrations hydrazine added into the NaOH solution. Fig. 7B shows that a good linear relationship can be obtained with a linear range of 0.99–5903 μM, a sensitivity of 4.45 μA mM−1 and the limit of detection (S/N = 3) of 0.024 μM. The current response with the addition of hydrazine at lower concentration is shown in the inset of Fig. 7B. This indicates the high current response of CDs-Cu2O/CuO-GCE for hydrazine in NaOH solution. A comparison of CDs-Cu2O/CuO-GCE with other modified electrodes is shown in Table 1 .

The current response for hydrazine after 1 month of storage in 4 °C still maintains 99.52% of the original electrode (Fig. S9), and the current response for hydrazine after 7 days of storage in 37 °C still maintains 97.60% of the original electrode (Fig. S10), which show that CDs-Cu2O/CuO-GCE has a good stability. The reproducibility of the current response of hydrazine on the modified electrode was also investigated (Fig. S11). The same electrochemical sensor was re-built 10 times using the same working electrode. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.42% were obtained, which indicated the sensor has excellent reproducibility. The current response of several substances that are 10-folds higher than the concentration of hydrazine (0.01 M) in the process of detecting hydrazine were also studied, and observed the interfering substance have little response in Fig. 8 . It illustrates that CDs-Cu2O/CuO has excellent anti-interference ability in the detection of hydrazine.

In summary, CDs were synthesized using the simple one-step hydrothermal method from reed, and the average diameter of CDs was obtained to be 2.7 nm. The novel nanocomposite of CDs-Cu2O/CuO was synthesized by a simple one-step ultrasonic method using reed-based CDs as reducing agent for electrochemical determination of hydrazine. CDs-Cu2O/CuO shows a significant improvement in electrocatalytic hydrazine with a wide linear range of 0.99–5903 μM, a sensitivity of 4.45 μA mM−1 and the limit of detection is 0.024 μM and good stability. The prepared composite not only make full use of agricultural waste-reed, but also instead of expensive electrode materials and provide new idea for the processes of synthesis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the following funders for financial support: Higher Education Science and Technology Research Project of Hebei Province, China (No.ZD2019022); National Science and Technology Major Projects (No. 2018ZX0711000404); Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (No. B2019201407).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reed leaf-inspired graphene films with anisotropic superhydrophobicity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.B. Jiang, Y.Q. Liu, Y.L. Zhang, Y. Liu, X.Y. Fu, D.D. Han, Y.Y. Song, L. Ren, H.B. Sun, Reed leaf-inspired graphene films with anisotropic superhydrophobicity, Acs Appl. Mater. Interfaces 10 (2018), pp. 18416-1825.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simple synthesis of CuO/Ag nanocomposite electrode using precursor ink for non-enzymatic electrochemical hydrogen peroxide sensing

sb:title.sb:maintitle: CuO/Cu2O composite hollow polyhedrons fabricated from metal–organic framework templates for lithium-ion battery anodes with a long cycling life

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Hu, Y. Huang, F. Zhang, Q. Chen, CuO/Cu2O composite hollow polyhedrons fabricated from metal-organic framework templates for lithium-ion battery anodes with a long cycling life, Nanoscale 5 (2013), pp. 4186-4190.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: MOF-templated formation of porous CuO hollow octahedra for lithium-ion battery anode materials

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemical Deposition of Cu2O Nanocrystals with Precise Morphology Control

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.D. Susman, Y. Feldman, A. Vaskevich, I. Rubinstein, Chemical deposition of Cu2O nanocrystals with precise morphology control, Acs. Nano8 (2014), pp. 162-174.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nearly monodisperse Cu2O and CuO nanospheres: preparation and applications for sensitive gas sensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.T. Zhang, J.F. Liu, Q. Peng, X. Wang, Y.D. Li, Nearly monodisperse Cu2O and CuO nanospheres: preparation and applications for sensitive gas sensors, Chem. Mater. 18 (2006), pp. 867-871.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of cuprous oxides with different sizes and their behaviors of adsorption, visible-light driven photocatalysis and photocorrosion

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Huang, F. Peng, H. Yu, H. Wang, Preparation of cuprous oxides with different sizes and their behaviors of adsorption, visible-light driven photocatalysis and photocorrosion,Solid State Sci. 11 (2009), pp. 129-138.

Acta. 56 (2010), pp. 953-958.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical study of hydrazine oxidation by leaf-shaped copper oxide loaded on highly ordered mesoporous carbon composite

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Wang, T. Meng, H. Jia, Y. Feng, T. Gong, H. Wang, Y. Zhang, Electrochemical study of hydrazine oxidation by leaf-shaped copper oxide loaded on highly ordered mesoporous carbon composite, J. Colloid Interface Sci. 549 (2019), pp. 98-104.
